skill.name: use-my-browser
skill.description: Guides agents through browser automation, live Chrome DevTools inspection, public-web research, raw fetches, and clean browser fallback choices.
skill.summary: Choose the right browsing layer for web research, DevTools handoff, and live browser automation.
skill.author: xixu-me

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many alerts because Markdown examples and tool names were parsed as executable or cryptographic patterns. Review found no bundled scripts, credential exfiltration, prompt injection, or malicious intent, but the skill has legitimate elevated risk because it guides live authenticated browser access, DevTools script evaluation, raw fetches, and file upload or save workflows.

medium_findings.title: Live Browser Session Can Expose Authenticated State
medium_findings.description: The skill explicitly routes agents to reuse the current Chrome session for logged-in pages, cookies, selected DevTools context, DOM data, network activity, and rendered evidence. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but it can expose sensitive pages and session-backed data if used without clear user consent and careful tab hygiene.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed text directly describes reuse of signed-in browser state and warns that live-session access can expose open windows. The risk is inherent to the documented capability, not inferred from hidden code.

medium_findings.title: DevTools Script Evaluation and Raw Fetch Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends evaluate_script for page inspection and shell-based fetch tools for raw HTML, headers, JSON-LD, and direct assets. These operations are useful for debugging, but they create network and code-execution surfaces that can read page data or retrieve external content.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are documented tool-routing guidance, not bundled executable code. The security concern is still clear because the documented workflow includes arbitrary page script evaluation and shell fetch operations.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Alerts Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported many Ruby or shell backtick executions in README.md, README.zh.md, SKILL.md, and reference files. Review shows these are Markdown inline code names, command examples, links, or tool identifiers rather than executable Ruby backticks embedded in a script.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown documentation and tables, not source files that execute backtick commands. The skill package contains policy and reference files, with no script file found in the scanned structure.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography and Obfuscation Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alerts appear to come from ordinary prose, names, or Markdown content, and the high entropy alert is explained by the Chinese translation file. No evidence found of cryptographic code, packed payloads, encoded command strings, or concealed executable content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found documentation text and translated prose at the flagged locations. The confidence is high, although the exact static token that triggered each weak-crypto alert is not provided.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: A targeted search for override language, fake system instructions, pre-approval claims, and requests to skip security review found no evidence of prompt injection in the reviewed skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Searches covered the scanned files for common injection phrases and manual review found ordinary safety guidance. This does not prove absence of every possible injection phrase, but no evidence was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Authenticated Browser Automation Surface
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill can steer agents toward Chrome DevTools access to signed-in sessions, selected elements, selected network requests, console output, and current page state. Marketplace users should understand that this is a powerful browser-control workflow.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Multiple files document live browser and DevTools access as a core capability. The pattern is legitimate but sensitive because it can reach authenticated user context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Arbitrary Page Script Evaluation Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The reference material maps page inspection to evaluate_script and recommends it when data exists in a rendered page. This can read or manipulate page context if used carelessly.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines directly identify evaluate_script as an inspection and scrolling mechanism. The concern is about capability exposure, not evidence of malicious implementation.

content.value_statement: Web tasks often need different access paths, from public sources to a signed-in browser session. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code choose the least intrusive browser workflow that can still solve the task.
actual_capabilities: Routes tasks between public web tools, processed reads, raw fetches, Chrome DevTools, and Playwright-style clean browser contexts.
Guides safe use of a logged-in Chrome session when cookies, current app state, or selected DevTools context matters.
Explains how to inspect DOM snapshots, network requests, console messages, screenshots, performance traces, and page scripts.
Provides recipes for tab control, interaction, scrolling, waiting, media extraction, uploads, and Lighthouse-style audits.
Supports reusable site notes for confirmed domain patterns, login requirements, extraction tactics, and known traps.
limitations: It is a strategy and documentation skill, not a standalone browser automation runtime.
Live browser work depends on available Chrome DevTools, web, shell, or Playwright tools in the host environment.
Authenticated session access can expose sensitive pages, cookies, requests, and user data if used without care.
It does not guarantee that anti-bot-heavy, private, or rapidly changing sites will be reachable.

use_cases.title: Debug a Signed-In Web App
use_cases.description: Continue from the current Chrome tab or selected DevTools request to inspect DOM state, failed requests, console errors, and rendering issues.

use_cases.title: Research Dynamic Public Pages
use_cases.description: Start with public sources, then escalate to rendered browser inspection when search results or static reads miss dynamic content.

use_cases.title: Extract Real Page Assets
use_cases.description: Find image, video, or metadata sources from lazy-loaded pages while avoiding unnecessary screenshots or intrusive browser actions.

prompt_templates.title: Choose the Browser Path
prompt_templates.prompt: Use my-browser strategy for this page inspection task. First decide whether public web, raw fetch, live Chrome DevTools, or a clean browser context is the right starting point.

prompt_templates.title: Inspect My Current Tab
prompt_templates.prompt: I have the relevant page open in Chrome. Use the live browser session only as needed, preserve my current tab state, and explain the evidence you find.

prompt_templates.title: Debug a Selected Request
prompt_templates.prompt: I selected a failing request in DevTools. Inspect that request first, then compare nearby network activity only if the selected request is not enough.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Several Sites
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare these sites using the cheapest reliable layer for each one. Batch public research where possible, and escalate to browser inspection only for dynamic or authenticated evidence.

output_examples.input: Check whether this dashboard bug is caused by the frontend or the API.
output_examples.output: The agent starts from the current signed-in tab, checks the selected request and console output, then reports the failing endpoint, response status, visible UI symptom, and next debugging step.

output_examples.input: Find the real video URL from this lazy-loaded article page.
output_examples.output: The agent reads the page structure first, inspects media elements and network requests, then provides the discovered asset source and notes whether a screenshot was unnecessary.

output_examples.input: Compare three public product pages and cite the sources.
output_examples.output: The agent begins with public web reads for citations, only opens a browser for pages that require rendering, and returns a concise comparison with source-backed claims.

best_practices: Define the success condition before choosing public web, raw fetch, live browser, or clean browser tooling.
Use the least intrusive layer that can answer the question, and escalate only when evidence shows it is needed.
Preserve the user session by avoiding unrelated tabs, destructive actions, and unnecessary live-browser interaction.
anti_patterns: Opening or modifying the user browser when public sources already answer the request.
Treating screenshots or not-found pages as final evidence before checking DOM, network, or source data.
Using raw shell fetches, DevTools scripts, or file uploads without confirming the goal and user intent.

faq.question: Does this skill control my browser by itself?
faq.answer: No. It gives routing and safety guidance for agents that already have browser, web, shell, or DevTools tools available.

faq.question: When should an agent use the live Chrome session?
faq.answer: Use it when the task depends on login state, cookies, current app context, selected DevTools data, or rendered dynamic behavior.

faq.question: Can it work without Chrome DevTools MCP?
faq.answer: Some public research and raw fetch workflows can still apply, but live-session handoff needs the relevant browser tooling.

faq.question: How does it reduce risk?
faq.answer: It tells the agent to start with lower-risk public or processed reads and to protect the current browser session during escalation.

faq.question: Can it inspect private or signed-in pages?
faq.answer: Yes, if the user grants access through the current browser session. This can expose sensitive data and should be used deliberately.

faq.question: Is this mainly for research or debugging?
faq.answer: It supports both. It helps with cited research, dynamic site inspection, authenticated debugging, media extraction, and DevTools handoff.
